MTN hikes dividend by 45% after profit rebound - Business Insider Africa MTN has raised its dividend by 45% after returning to profit in 2025, signalling renewed confidence in its financial position following a difficult prior year. MTN branding at a corporate office, as the telecom group raises dividends following a return to profitability. * MTN increased its dividend by 45% after returning to profit in 2025. * markets  MTN’s profits are rising, but Nigeria is expensive to run. * markets  Nigeria overtakes South Africa as MTN’s biggest profit engine. The increase comes after MTN swung back to profitability, driven by robust growth in Nigeria and Ghana, alongside improved operating performance across the Group. Jihadist violence in Nigeria and DRC rose sharply last year even as global deaths from terror fell - The Guardian # Jihadist violence in Nigeria and DRC rose sharply last year even as global deaths from terror fell. Nigeria had largest increase in terrorism-related deaths, ranking fourth in global index behind Pakistan, Burkina Faso and Niger. Jihadist violence rose sharply in Nigeria and Democratic Republic of Congo last year, even as global deaths from terrorism dropped to their lowest level in a decade, according to a new report. Nigeria recorded the largest increase in terrorism deaths globally in 2025, with fatalities rising by 46% from 513 in 2024 to 750, placing it fourth in the Global Terrorism Index, behind Pakistan, Burkina Faso and Niger. In the DRC, terrorism-related deaths rose by nearly 28% in 2025, increasing from 365 to 467 and pushing the central African state to eighth place on the index, its worst ranking. Burkina Faso, where the junta only controls about a third of the territory, recorded the largest decrease in terrorism deaths worldwide, with fatalities dropping by half in 2025. https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/mar/19/jihadist-violence-nigeria-drc-terrorism-index

Top 10 African countries the United States made the most money from last year - Business Insider Africa **In 2025, numerous African countries increased their trading links with the United States, exporting a sizable number of goods despite tariff conflicts, economic challenges,** **sanctions****, and shifting U.S.-Africa trade policy debates.**. * In 2025, many African countries strengthened trade with the United States despite ongoing tariff disputes and economic obstacles. * U.S. tariff changes and policy shifts created uncertainty for African exporters, affecting market access and contract negotiations. Overall, African exports to the United States remained important in 2025, with oil, precious metals, cocoa, cotton, and agricultural items taking the lead. Nigeria, Angola, Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, and South Africa were among the top African exporters to the United States, demonstrating a wide export basket that includes more than just crude oil. Despite these, some African countries were able to export more goods to the United States than others.
